projects.title: Multi-Agent Research & Blog System
projects.description: An autonomous AI agent pipeline built with CrewAI that coordinates specialized agents to perform deep web research and synthesize high-quality technical blog posts, significantly reducing content creation cycles.

projects.title: Workflow Automation Bot
projects.description: End-to-end automation pipeline connecting 12+ enterprise tools — eliminating 40+ hours of manual work per week across teams.

knowledge_center.title: LangChain memory not persisting across sessions
knowledge_center.summary: ConversationBufferMemory resets on every new chain call when not properly linked. Here's how to persist it using Redis or a simple file backend.
body: When using `ConversationBufferMemory` in LangChain, memory is stored in-process and is lost when the Python process restarts.

**Fix:** Use `RedisChatMessageHistory` to persist memory across sessions.

```python
from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferMemory
from langchain.memory.chat_message_histories import RedisChatMessageHistory

history = RedisChatMessageHistory(session_id='user-123', url='redis://localhost:6379')
memory = ConversationBufferMemory(chat_memory=history)
```
Make sure your Redis instance is running and the `redis` Python package is installed (`pip install redis`).

knowledge_center.title: Speed up n8n workflows with parallel branches
knowledge_center.summary: Running tasks sequentially in n8n is fine for simple flows, but parallel branches cut execution time dramatically for independent tasks.
body: In n8n, every node by default runs after the previous one finishes. If you have two API calls that don't depend on each other, you're wasting time.
**The trick:** Use a `Split In Batches` node or duplicate connections from a single node to two independent branches. Both branches execute simultaneously.

For example: after fetching a list of users, send Slack notifications AND update your CRM at the same time instead of one after the other. I saw a 3x speed improvement on a workflow that was previously taking 45 seconds.

knowledge_center.title: OpenAI Function Calling → now called Tool Calling
knowledge_center.summary: OpenAI rebranded 'function calling' to 'tool calling' in their API. If you're using older LangChain code, here's what changed and what to update.
body: As of OpenAI API v1.1+, the `functions` parameter in chat completions is deprecated in favor of `tools`. LangChain updated their wrappers to reflect this.
**What to update:** Replace `llm.predict_messages(... functions=...)` with the new `bind_tools()` method on your chat model.

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.tools import tool

@tool
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    return f'Weather in {city}: sunny'

llm = ChatOpenAI().bind_tools([get_weather])
```
The new approach is cleaner and works across different model providers without changing your tool definitions.

knowledge_center.title: Shrink prompt token usage with structured output instead of verbose instructions
knowledge_center.summary: Long instruction blocks telling the model 'respond only in JSON with these fields' waste tokens. Use the response_format parameter instead.
body: A common mistake is writing 5–8 lines of system prompt instructing the model on output format when the API has a native way to enforce it.

**Trick:** Use `response_format={ 'type': 'json_object' }` on GPT-4o and newer models to enforce JSON output without burning tokens on formatting instructions.

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    response_format={ 'type': 'json_object' },
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'List 3 cities with their population'}]
)
```
This alone can save 30–60 tokens per call depending on how verbose your previous format instructions were. At scale, that compounds fast.

knowledge_center.title: Use system prompt caching to cut costs on repeated agent calls
knowledge_center.summary: If your agent always starts with a large system prompt (tools list, persona, context), Anthropic's prompt caching can reduce cost by up to 90% on that portion.
body: In multi-turn agent loops, the system prompt is resent with every call. If it's 2000 tokens and you make 50 calls, that's 100,000 tokens — most of it redundant.
Cached tokens are billed at ~10% of normal input token price. For agents with large static contexts (RAG chunks, tool schemas), this is a huge cost lever.

knowledge_center.title: Always set max_tokens explicitly to avoid runaway generation costs
knowledge_center.summary: Without a max_tokens limit, models can generate thousands of tokens for a simple task. Always cap output to what you actually need.
body: Leaving `max_tokens` unset is one of the most common silent cost drains in production AI systems. The model will generate until it hits its context limit.

**Tip:** Estimate the worst-case output size for your task and set `max_tokens` to 1.5x that. For classification tasks, set it as low as 10–20.

```python
# For a yes/no classifier — no reason to allow 2000 tokens
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    max_tokens=5,
    messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': 'Is this spam? Reply Yes or No.\n\n' + email_text}]
)
```
Pairing a small model (gpt-4o-mini) with a tight `max_tokens` for simple classification tasks can reduce cost by 95% compared to using GPT-4o with no limit.

knowledge_center.title: Use tiktoken to pre-count tokens before sending requests
knowledge_center.summary: Don't guess how many tokens your prompt uses. Pre-count with tiktoken and gate expensive calls behind a token budget check.
body: Sending oversized prompts to the API wastes money and can hit context limits silently. Pre-counting lets you truncate or summarize before the call.
This pattern is especially useful in RAG pipelines where retrieved chunks can balloon unpredictably depending on the query.

knowledge_center.title: Route tasks to smaller models for simple subtasks in an agent pipeline
knowledge_center.summary: Not every step in your agent needs GPT-4o. Routing simple classification or extraction steps to gpt-4o-mini saves significant cost.
body: In a multi-step agent, different steps have different complexity requirements. Using a powerful model for every step is like using a sledgehammer to crack a nut.
In a real project, routing ~60% of subtasks to gpt-4o-mini reduced monthly API costs by about 40% with no measurable quality drop.

knowledge_center.title: Compress conversation history with a rolling summary instead of full context
knowledge_center.summary: Keeping the full chat history in context grows linearly. Replace old messages with a running summary to keep token usage flat.
body: Multi-turn conversations balloon in token count fast. After 10 turns, you might be sending 3,000+ tokens of history just for context.
This keeps context size roughly constant no matter how long the conversation runs — critical for long-running support bots and agents.

knowledge_center.title: LangGraph is now the recommended way to build stateful agents in LangChain
knowledge_center.summary: LangChain's older AgentExecutor is being replaced by LangGraph for building agents with loops, branching, and persistent state.
body: If you're still using `initialize_agent()` or `AgentExecutor` from LangChain, you're on a deprecated path. LangGraph is the new standard for complex agents.
**What changed:** LangGraph models agent workflows as directed graphs with nodes (steps) and edges (transitions), giving you explicit control over loops and branching.

```python
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

builder = StateGraph(AgentState)
builder.add_node('call_llm', call_llm)
builder.add_node('run_tool', run_tool)
builder.add_conditional_edges('call_llm', should_continue, {'tool': 'run_tool', 'end': END})
graph = builder.compile()
```
The key benefit: you get checkpointing, human-in-the-loop pausing, and time-travel debugging out of the box — things AgentExecutor could never do cleanly.

knowledge_center.title: Use few-shot examples inside tool descriptions to improve tool selection
knowledge_center.summary: Agents often pick the wrong tool when instructions are vague. Adding 1–2 examples directly in the tool description dramatically improves selection accuracy.
body: Tool descriptions are what the LLM uses to decide which tool to call. Vague descriptions like 'searches the web' lead to wrong selections.

**Tip:** Write tool descriptions that include a concrete example of when to use it and when NOT to.

```python
@tool
def search_internal_docs(query: str) -> str:
    """
    Search internal company documentation.
    USE THIS for questions about company policies, onboarding, internal processes.
    DO NOT USE for general knowledge or current events — use web_search instead.
    Example: 'What is our refund policy?' → use this tool.
    """
    return vector_search(query)
```
This simple addition reduced incorrect tool calls by ~35% in a customer support agent I built, without any other changes.

knowledge_center.title: Agent stuck in a tool-calling loop — how to add a step limit
knowledge_center.summary: Agents without a step limit can loop infinitely when a tool returns unexpected output. Always set max_iterations.
body: An agent that can't solve a task gracefully will keep retrying tools in a loop, burning tokens and money until it hits the context limit.

**Fix:** Set `max_iterations` in LangChain's AgentExecutor, or implement a step counter in LangGraph.

```python
# LangChain AgentExecutor
agent_executor = AgentExecutor(
    agent=agent,
    tools=tools,
    max_iterations=10,
    early_stopping_method='generate'  # forces a final answer instead of just stopping
)
```
Also add a fallback message so the user knows the agent hit its limit: `handle_parsing_errors=True` prevents raw exceptions from surfacing.

knowledge_center.title: Strip whitespace and redundant phrasing from prompts to save tokens
knowledge_center.summary: Verbose system prompts with filler phrases, double spaces, and repeated context are silent token drains. A quick cleanup script can trim 10–20% of tokens.
body: Many system prompts accumulate bloat over time — phrases like 'Please make sure to always...', 'It is very important that you...', and repeated context blocks.

**Trick:** Run your prompts through a cleanup function before every call.

```python
import re

def slim_prompt(text: str) -> str:
    # collapse multiple spaces/newlines
    text = re.sub(r'\n{3,}', '\n\n', text)
    text = re.sub(r' {2,}', ' ', text)
    # remove filler phrases
    fillers = ['Please make sure to', 'It is very important that', 'Always remember to']
    for f in fillers:
        text = text.replace(f, '')
    return text.strip()
```
On a 1,500-token system prompt, this consistently shaved 150–250 tokens — about 10–15% reduction for zero quality loss.

knowledge_center.title: Use semantic chunking instead of fixed-size chunking for better RAG results
knowledge_center.summary: Fixed 512-token chunks often split ideas mid-thought. Semantic chunking groups related sentences together, dramatically improving retrieval quality.
body: The default `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter` in LangChain splits by character count, which can break a single concept across two chunks — meaning neither chunk retrieves well.

**Tip:** Use `SemanticChunker` from LangChain Experimental which groups sentences by embedding similarity.

```python
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

chunker = SemanticChunker(
    OpenAIEmbeddings(),
    breakpoint_threshold_type='percentile'
)
docs = chunker.create_documents([your_text])
```
In a knowledge base of 500 technical documents, switching to semantic chunking improved answer accuracy from ~67% to ~84% on our internal eval set.

knowledge_center.title: Add a reranker after vector search to fix RAG relevance issues
knowledge_center.summary: Vector similarity search returns the most 'similar' chunks, not the most relevant ones. A cross-encoder reranker fixes this with minimal overhead.
body: Cosine similarity on embeddings is fast but imprecise — chunks that share vocabulary with the query score high even if they don't answer it.

**Trick:** After retrieving top-20 candidates, rerank with a cross-encoder and only pass the top-5 to the LLM.

```python
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

reranker = CrossEncoder('cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2')

def rerank(query: str, candidates: list) -> list:
    pairs = [(query, c.page_content) for c in candidates]
    scores = reranker.predict(pairs)
    ranked = sorted(zip(scores, candidates), reverse=True)
    return [doc for _, doc in ranked[:5]]
```
This halves the tokens sent to the LLM (top-5 vs top-10) while simultaneously improving answer quality. Win-win.

knowledge_center.title: CrewAI now supports async agent execution natively
knowledge_center.summary: CrewAI v0.51+ added `kickoff_async()` which lets you run crews without blocking your main thread — critical for production web applications.
body: Earlier versions of CrewAI only offered synchronous `kickoff()`, which blocked the event loop in async frameworks like FastAPI.

**What's new:** `kickoff_async()` lets you await the crew result without blocking.

```python
import asyncio
from crewai import Crew

async def run_research(topic: str):
    crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, writer], tasks=[research_task, write_task])
    result = await crew.kickoff_async(inputs={'topic': topic})
    return result

# In FastAPI
@app.post('/research')
async def research_endpoint(topic: str):
    result = await run_research(topic)
    return {'result': result}
```
This makes CrewAI usable in production APIs without spinning up separate threads or processes for each crew run.

knowledge_center.title: Pass only relevant document fields to the LLM, not entire records
knowledge_center.summary: When using tools that fetch database records, sending the full JSON object burns tokens. Extract only the fields the LLM needs for the task.
body: If your agent fetches a customer record with 30 fields but only needs name, email, and plan_type — you're wasting ~400 tokens per call on irrelevant data.

**Tip:** Create a slim projection of your data before inserting it into the prompt.

```python
def slim_customer(customer: dict) -> dict:
    return {
        'name': customer['full_name'],
        'email': customer['email'],
        'plan': customer['subscription']['plan_type'],
        'active': customer['status'] == 'active'
    }

# In your tool
prompt = f'Customer info: {slim_customer(raw_record)}'
```
This single pattern reduced per-call token usage by 35% in a CRM agent that fetched full Salesforce records.

knowledge_center.title: Pydantic validation errors crashing your LangChain output parser
knowledge_center.summary: When the LLM returns slightly malformed JSON, Pydantic parsers crash hard. Here's a robust fallback pattern using OutputFixingParser.
body: LLMs sometimes produce JSON with trailing commas, missing quotes, or extra text — all of which break `PydanticOutputParser` with a hard error.

**Fix:** Wrap your parser with `OutputFixingParser` which sends the broken output back to the LLM for repair automatically.

```python
from langchain.output_parsers import PydanticOutputParser, OutputFixingParser
from pydantic import BaseModel

class TaskResult(BaseModel):
    summary: str
    confidence: float

base_parser = PydanticOutputParser(pydantic_object=TaskResult)
robust_parser = OutputFixingParser.from_llm(parser=base_parser, llm=llm)

# This will auto-fix malformed output before parsing
result = robust_parser.parse(llm_output)
```
The fix adds one extra LLM call only when needed — a good trade-off versus a crashed pipeline.

knowledge_center.title: Use hypothetical document embeddings (HyDE) to improve sparse query retrieval
knowledge_center.summary: Short or vague queries retrieve poorly from vector stores. HyDE generates a hypothetical answer first, then uses that richer text for embedding — massively improving recall.
body: A query like 'refund?' is hard to embed well. The vector is sparse and won't match relevant chunks that use words like 'return policy' or 'reimbursement'.

**Trick:** Use HyDE — ask the LLM to generate a hypothetical document that would answer the query, then embed that instead.

```python
def hyde_retrieve(query: str, vectorstore, llm) -> list:
    # Step 1: generate a hypothetical answer
    hypothetical = llm.invoke(
        f'Write a short paragraph that would answer this question: {query}'
    )
    # Step 2: embed the hypothetical answer, not the query
    results = vectorstore.similarity_search(hypothetical, k=5)
    return results
```
HyDE typically improves retrieval recall by 15–30% on short or ambiguous queries. The extra LLM call is cheap if you use a small model for the hypothesis step.

knowledge_center.title: Instrument your agents with LangSmith for production debugging
knowledge_center.summary: When agents fail in production, you need a trace of every LLM call, tool invocation, and token count. LangSmith provides this out of the box with one env variable.
body: Debugging a failed agent run from logs alone is painful — you can't see what the model was thinking, which tool was called, or how tokens were spent.

**Tip:** Enable LangSmith tracing with two environment variables — no code changes needed.

```bash
export LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=true
export LANGCHAIN_API_KEY=your_key_here
export LANGCHAIN_PROJECT=my-agent-prod
```

Every run now appears in the LangSmith dashboard with a full trace: inputs, outputs, latency, token counts, and cost per step. This paid for itself the first time we caught a prompt regression in production within minutes.

knowledge_center.title: Batch embedding API calls instead of embedding one chunk at a time
knowledge_center.summary: Calling the embeddings API for each chunk individually is slow and expensive. Batch them into a single request to reduce latency by 10x.
body: A common anti-pattern in RAG pipelines is a for-loop that calls `embeddings.embed_query(chunk)` one at a time. This creates N API round-trips instead of 1.

**Trick:** Use `embed_documents()` which accepts a list and sends a single batched request.

```python
# Slow — N API calls
embeddings_list = [embeddings.embed_query(chunk) for chunk in chunks]

# Fast — 1 API call
embeddings_list = embeddings.embed_documents(chunks)
```
For 500 chunks, this reduced embedding time from ~45 seconds to ~4 seconds in my pipeline. The OpenAI embeddings API accepts up to 2048 inputs per request.

knowledge_center.title: Mistral AI released Mistral Small 3.1 — beats GPT-4o-mini on most benchmarks
knowledge_center.summary: Mistral Small 3.1 (24B parameters) is now one of the best small models available, with better reasoning than GPT-4o-mini at a lower price per token.
body: As of early 2025, Mistral Small 3.1 outperforms GPT-4o-mini on MMLU, HumanEval, and GSM8K while being cheaper per million tokens.
**What this means for agents:** If you've been using GPT-4o-mini for routing, classification, or extraction subtasks, Mistral Small 3.1 is worth benchmarking as a drop-in replacement.

```python
from langchain_mistralai import ChatMistralAI

llm = ChatMistralAI(
    model='mistral-small-latest',
    temperature=0
)
```
The LangChain integration works identically to OpenAI's — you can swap models with a single line change and run your eval suite to compare.

knowledge_center.title: Use temperature=0 for deterministic agent tool calls
knowledge_center.summary: Agent tool-calling steps should almost always use temperature=0. Randomness in tool selection leads to inconsistent, hard-to-debug behavior.
body: Temperature controls randomness. For creative tasks (writing, brainstorming), higher values help. For tool-calling in agents, randomness is your enemy — it causes the same input to trigger different tools on different runs.

**Tip:** Set `temperature=0` globally for agent chains, and only raise it for specific generation steps that need creativity.

```python
# Agent planner — needs determinism
planner_llm = ChatOpenAI(model='gpt-4o', temperature=0)

# Content generator — benefits from variety
writer_llm = ChatOpenAI(model='gpt-4o', temperature=0.7)
```
This split-temperature pattern makes your agent's structural behavior reproducible while still allowing creative output where it matters.

knowledge_center.title: Context window overflow crashing long RAG chains
knowledge_center.summary: When too many chunks are retrieved, the full prompt exceeds the model's context window and the API returns a 400 error. Here's how to guard against it.
body: RAG pipelines that retrieve top-10 chunks can easily push your total prompt over 8K or 16K tokens depending on chunk size, causing a hard API error.

**Fix:** Measure the assembled prompt's token count before sending and truncate retrieved docs if needed.

```python
import tiktoken

def safe_rag_prompt(query: str, docs: list, max_tokens: int = 6000) -> str:
    enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model('gpt-4o')
    context = ''
    for doc in docs:
        candidate = context + doc.page_content + '\n'
        if len(enc.encode(candidate)) > max_tokens:
            break
        context = candidate
    return f'Context:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {query}'
```
This ensures you use as much context as possible without ever exceeding the limit. Always leave 1000–2000 tokens headroom for the output.

knowledge_center.title: Store agent scratchpad observations externally to keep context lean
knowledge_center.summary: ReAct agents accumulate tool observations in-context. Storing them externally and passing only a summary keeps the active context window small.
body: In ReAct-style agents, each tool call result is appended to the conversation. After 5–6 tool calls the context is dominated by raw tool outputs, not the actual task.

**Trick:** Intercept tool results, store them in a dict, and pass only a compressed reference into the LLM context.

```python
observation_store = {}

def tool_with_external_memory(tool_name: str, result: str) -> str:
    key = f'obs_{len(observation_store)}'
    observation_store[key] = result
    # Return a compact reference instead of the full result
    preview = result[:200] + '...' if len(result) > 200 else result
    return f'[{key}] {preview}'
```
When the agent needs the full result later, it can request it by key. This pattern keeps context flat regardless of how many tools are called.

knowledge_center.title: Use OpenAI's Batch API for non-real-time workloads to cut cost by 50%
knowledge_center.summary: The OpenAI Batch API processes requests asynchronously within 24 hours at 50% of the standard price — perfect for eval pipelines, bulk classification, and report generation.
body: If you're running bulk jobs — processing 10,000 documents overnight, running evals, or generating weekly reports — you're likely paying full price when you don't need to.

**Tip:** Use the Batch API for any non-real-time workload.

```python
from openai import OpenAI
import json

client = OpenAI()

# Create a .jsonl file with all your requests
requests = [
    {'custom_id': f'req-{i}', 'method': 'POST', 'url': '/v1/chat/completions',
     'body': {'model': 'gpt-4o-mini', 'messages': [{'role': 'user', 'content': doc}]}}
    for i, doc in enumerate(documents)
]

batch_file = client.files.create(file=json.dumps(requests).encode(), purpose='batch')
batch = client.batches.create(input_file_id=batch_file.id, endpoint='/v1/chat/completions', completion_window='24h')
```
50% cost reduction with zero quality difference. The only trade-off is latency — responses arrive within 24 hours, not milliseconds.

knowledge_center.title: Use XML tags in prompts for reliable structured output without JSON
knowledge_center.summary: Claude and some other models respond very reliably to XML-tagged output instructions — often more reliably than JSON format prompts, with no parsing overhead.
body: JSON output instructions can still result in the model adding preamble ('Sure! Here is the JSON: {...}') that breaks parsers. XML tags sidestep this.

**Trick:** Ask the model to wrap its output in custom XML tags, then extract with a simple regex.

```python
prompt = """
Analyze the sentiment of this review and respond ONLY with:
<sentiment>positive|neutral|negative</sentiment>
<confidence>0.0-1.0</confidence>

Review: {review}
"""

import re
response = llm.invoke(prompt)
sentiment = re.search(r'<sentiment>(.*?)</sentiment>', response).group(1)
confidence = float(re.search(r'<confidence>(.*?)</confidence>', response).group(1))
```
This pattern is especially effective with Claude, which has been trained extensively to respect XML structure in outputs.

knowledge_center.title: Give agents a 'thinking' step before tool use to reduce wrong calls
knowledge_center.summary: Adding an explicit inner-monologue step before tool selection — 'think step by step about which tool to use' — dramatically reduces incorrect tool invocations.
body: Agents that jump straight to tool selection often pick wrong. Adding a scratchpad-style reasoning step first forces the model to plan before acting.

**Tip:** Prefix your tool-calling prompt with a reasoning instruction.

```python
system_prompt = """
You are a helpful agent with access to tools.
Before calling any tool, output your reasoning in <think> tags:
<think>I need to find X, which means I should use tool Y because Z.</think>
Then proceed with the tool call.
"""
```
This one-line addition reduced incorrect tool calls by ~40% in an internal eval. The thinking tokens are a small overhead compared to the cost of a wrong tool call and retry.

knowledge_center.title: Pinecone now supports sparse-dense hybrid search natively
knowledge_center.summary: Pinecone's latest update adds built-in hybrid search — combining BM25 keyword matching with dense vector similarity in a single query, no custom code needed.
body: Pure dense vector search misses exact keyword matches. Pure BM25 misses semantic similarity. Hybrid search combines both — and Pinecone now does it natively.

**What's new:** Set `sparse_values` alongside your dense vector in upserts, and pass both in queries.

```python
from pinecone_text.sparse import BM25Encoder

bm25 = BM25Encoder().default()
sparse_vec = bm25.encode_documents([chunk_text])[0]

index.upsert(vectors=[{
    'id': chunk_id,
    'values': dense_embedding,
    'sparse_values': sparse_vec,
    'metadata': {'text': chunk_text}
}])

# Query with both
results = index.query(
    vector=query_dense,
    sparse_vector=bm25.encode_queries([query])[0],
    top_k=10
)
```
In benchmarks on technical documentation, hybrid search improved recall@5 from 71% to 89% over dense-only search.

knowledge_center.title: Cache LLM responses for identical inputs using a simple hash map
knowledge_center.summary: Many agent pipelines call the LLM with the same prompt repeatedly. A hash-based cache serves repeated calls instantly at zero cost.
body: In agent evaluation loops, document processing pipelines, and development, the same prompt is often sent to the LLM dozens of times. Every repeat call costs money.

**Trick:** Wrap your LLM call with a hash-based cache.

```python
import hashlib, json
from functools import lru_cache

cache = {}

def cached_llm_call(prompt: str, model: str = 'gpt-4o-mini') -> str:
    key = hashlib.md5((model + prompt).encode()).hexdigest()
    if key in cache:
        return cache[key]
    result = llm.invoke(prompt)
    cache[key] = result
    return result
```
For persistent caching across restarts, swap the dict for Redis or SQLite. LangChain also has a built-in `set_llm_cache()` function that does this globally.

knowledge_center.title: Use parent-child chunking for better RAG context without token bloat
knowledge_center.summary: Retrieve small chunks for precision, but pass the parent (larger) chunk to the LLM for full context. You get the best of both worlds.
body: Small chunks (128 tokens) retrieve precisely. Large chunks (512 tokens) provide the context the LLM needs to answer well. Parent-child chunking gives you both.

**Tip:** Use LangChain's `ParentDocumentRetriever` which stores small child chunks in the vector store but returns their large parent chunks to the LLM.

```python
from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

child_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=200)
parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=800)

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=InMemoryStore(),
    child_splitter=child_splitter,
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter
)
```
This improved my QA system's answer completeness significantly — small chunks matched user queries precisely while parents gave the LLM enough context to form complete answers.

knowledge_center.title: Embeddings returning cosine similarity of 1.0 for all vectors
knowledge_center.summary: If every document retrieves as equally similar, your vectors are likely all zeros or unnormalized. Here's how to diagnose and fix it.
body: When a vector store returns the same top-k documents regardless of the query, the embeddings are broken — usually all-zero vectors or a configuration error.

**Fix:** Diagnose first by inspecting a raw embedding.

```python
test_vec = embeddings.embed_query('test query')
print(f'Vector length: {len(test_vec)}')
print(f'Non-zero dims: {sum(1 for v in test_vec if v != 0)}')
print(f'Norm: {sum(v**2 for v in test_vec) ** 0.5:.4f}')
```

If norm is 0 or all dims are zero, check your API key, model name, and whether the embedding provider is rate-limiting you silently. If norm is non-zero but similarity is still 1.0, your vector store may not be normalizing on upsert — check the `metric` setting (should be `cosine`, not `dotproduct` unless you normalize manually).

knowledge_center.title: Use chain-of-thought prompting only when reasoning is needed — skip it otherwise
knowledge_center.summary: Chain-of-thought adds 100–500 tokens of output for reasoning. For simple classification or extraction tasks, it's pure overhead. Know when to skip it.
body: Chain-of-thought (CoT) is powerful for complex reasoning but expensive for simple tasks. Adding 'think step by step' to a sentiment classifier doubles your output tokens for no gain.

**Trick:** Use a task complexity router to decide when to use CoT.

```python
SIMPLE_TASKS = ['classify', 'extract', 'reformat', 'yes_no']
COMPLEX_TASKS = ['reason', 'plan', 'debug', 'analyze', 'compare']

def build_prompt(task_type: str, content: str) -> str:
    cot_instruction = 'Think step by step before answering.' if task_type in COMPLEX_TASKS else ''
    return f'{cot_instruction}\n\n{content}'.strip()
```
Applying this in a multi-task agent reduced average output tokens per call by 28% — significant at scale.

knowledge_center.title: Design multi-agent systems with a supervisor pattern, not a flat mesh
knowledge_center.summary: Agents that talk to each other freely become undebuggable. Use a supervisor agent to route, delegate, and validate — keeping communication structured.
body: A flat mesh of agents where any agent can call any other creates spaghetti workflows that are impossible to trace or debug when something goes wrong.
This pattern maps cleanly to LangGraph — the supervisor is a node with conditional edges to each worker. Every message passes through one point, making traces readable.

knowledge_center.title: Anthropic released Claude 3.5 Sonnet with 200K context — best for long-document agents
knowledge_center.summary: Claude 3.5 Sonnet now supports 200K token context windows, making it the top choice for agents that process entire codebases, contracts, or report sets.
body: Most models cap at 128K tokens. Claude 3.5 Sonnet's 200K context means you can fit ~150,000 words — roughly an entire novel — in a single call without chunking.
**What this means for RAG:** For some use cases, you can skip chunking entirely and pass the full document to the model. This eliminates retrieval errors at the cost of higher token spend per call.

```python
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()
message = client.messages.create(
    model='claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022',
    max_tokens=4096,
    messages=[{
        'role': 'user',
        'content': f'Analyze this entire contract and flag any unusual clauses:\n\n{full_contract_text}'
    }]
)
```
Best used for contracts, codebases, and research papers where losing any chunk to retrieval errors is costly.

knowledge_center.title: Dynamically build tool lists based on user intent to reduce schema token cost
knowledge_center.summary: Sending all 20 tools to the LLM every call wastes tokens on tool schemas the model will never use. Dynamically select the relevant 3–5 tools per request.
body: Every tool you give an agent has a schema that gets serialized into the prompt — typically 100–300 tokens per tool. With 20 tools, that's 2,000–6,000 tokens before you've even described the task.

**Trick:** Use a fast classifier to predict which tools are relevant, then pass only those.

```python
ALL_TOOLS = [web_search, calendar_tool, email_tool, crm_tool, db_query, ...]

def select_tools(user_message: str) -> list:
    # Fast classification — use a small model or keyword match
    relevant = keyword_router(user_message)  # returns ['web_search', 'db_query']
    return [t for t in ALL_TOOLS if t.name in relevant]

selected_tools = select_tools(user_message)
agent = create_agent(llm, selected_tools)
```
Cutting from 15 tools to 4 relevant ones saves ~2,000 tokens per call. At 10,000 calls/day, that's 20M tokens/day — a significant cost line.

knowledge_center.title: CrewAI agents ignoring task instructions and going off-script
knowledge_center.summary: CrewAI agents sometimes ignore task descriptions and make up their own objectives. The fix is explicit expected_output and tighter role definitions.
body: When a CrewAI agent's task description is too open-ended, the model fills in the blanks — often not in the direction you want.

**Fix:** Always set `expected_output` in your Task definition. This acts as a completion criterion that keeps the agent on-target.

```python
from crewai import Task

research_task = Task(
    description='Research the top 5 competitors of {company} and their pricing models.',
    expected_output=(
        'A structured list of 5 competitors, each with: company name, '
        'pricing tier names, price range, and key differentiators. '
        'No narrative. Bullet points only.'
    ),
    agent=researcher
)
```
Vague tasks get vague outputs. Precise `expected_output` strings act as implicit evaluation criteria the agent tries to satisfy.

knowledge_center.title: Use streaming for agent responses to improve perceived performance
knowledge_center.summary: Streaming lets users see output as it generates, which makes a 10-second wait feel much shorter than a 10-second blank screen.
body: For agents with long response times, the user experience of waiting for a complete response is significantly worse than seeing tokens stream in progressively.

**Tip:** Enable streaming on your LLM calls and pipe it through to your UI.

```python
# With OpenAI
for chunk in client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=messages,
    stream=True
):
    content = chunk.choices[0].delta.content
    if content:
        print(content, end='', flush=True)
```
In LangChain, use `.stream()` instead of `.invoke()` on any chain or LLM. For FastAPI, return a `StreamingResponse` — this pattern makes even slow agents feel responsive.

knowledge_center.title: Use sentence-level chunking for FAQ and Q&A datasets
knowledge_center.summary: For documents that are naturally Q&A structured, chunking by question-answer pair (not by token count) gives near-perfect retrieval alignment.
body: Generic token-based chunking breaks FAQ documents in the worst possible way — often splitting the question from its answer across chunk boundaries.

**Trick:** For FAQ-style content, parse and chunk explicitly by Q&A pair.

```python
import re

def chunk_faq(text: str) -> list:
    # Match 'Q: ...' and 'A: ...' patterns
    pattern = r'(Q:.*?)(A:.*?)(?=Q:|$)'
    pairs = re.findall(pattern, text, re.DOTALL)
    return [{'question': q.strip(), 'answer': a.strip(), 'chunk': q+a} for q, a in pairs]

chunks = chunk_faq(faq_document)
```
Each chunk is exactly one Q&A pair. Retrieval becomes trivially accurate because the embedding of a user's question matches the stored question directly.

knowledge_center.title: AutoGen v0.4 released with a complete rewrite — async-first architecture
knowledge_center.summary: Microsoft's AutoGen v0.4 is a ground-up rewrite with native async support, a cleaner agent API, and better integration with external tools and human-in-the-loop flows.
body: AutoGen v0.4 is not backward compatible with v0.2. The new architecture is built around an async message-passing model which makes it significantly more scalable.

**Key changes:** Agents are now defined via `AssistantAgent` and `UserProxyAgent` with explicit `on_messages` handlers, and all communication is async.

```python
from autogen_agentchat.agents import AssistantAgent
from autogen_ext.models import OpenAIChatCompletionClient

model_client = OpenAIChatCompletionClient(model='gpt-4o')
agent = AssistantAgent(name='assistant', model_client=model_client)

# Async conversation
result = await agent.run(task='What is 2+2?')
print(result.messages)
```
If you're starting a new multi-agent project, build on v0.4. If you have v0.2 code in production, the migration guide is available on the AutoGen GitHub.

knowledge_center.title: Add metadata filters to vector search to avoid cross-tenant data leaks
knowledge_center.summary: In multi-tenant RAG systems, vector similarity alone doesn't respect user boundaries. Always filter by tenant_id metadata to prevent data leakage.
body: A common security gap in multi-tenant RAG: if you only filter by similarity, a query from User A might retrieve User B's documents if they're topically similar.

**Tip:** Always include a metadata filter on `tenant_id` (or `user_id`) in every vector search call.

```python
# Without filter — DANGEROUS in multi-tenant systems
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=5)

# Safe — filter by tenant
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    query,
    k=5,
    filter={'tenant_id': current_user.tenant_id}
)
```
This applies to Pinecone (use `filter=` param), Chroma (use `where=`), and Weaviate (use `where` filter). Never assume similarity search is implicitly scoped.

knowledge_center.title: Log token usage per agent step to find where your budget is going
knowledge_center.summary: Most teams don't know which step in their agent pipeline consumes the most tokens. Adding per-step logging takes 10 minutes and reveals easy optimization targets.
body: Without per-step token logging, optimizing an agent pipeline is guesswork. You might spend hours optimizing a step that uses 200 tokens while ignoring one that uses 4,000.

**Trick:** Extract token usage from the LLM response and log it per step.

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4o', messages=messages)

usage = response.usage
print(f'[Step: {step_name}] Prompt: {usage.prompt_tokens} | Completion: {usage.completion_tokens} | Total: {usage.total_tokens}')
```
After adding this to a 6-step agent, I discovered one summarization step was consuming 60% of all tokens. Replacing it with a smaller model for that step alone cut total cost by 40%.

knowledge_center.title: Pinecone upserts silently failing on vectors with NaN values
knowledge_center.summary: If your embedding model returns NaN values for certain inputs (empty strings, special characters), Pinecone silently drops those upserts. Here's how to catch and fix it.
body: Empty strings, strings with only punctuation, or very short inputs can cause embedding models to return NaN or zero vectors — and Pinecone silently discards them on upsert.
Also filter out empty or whitespace-only text before embedding to prevent the issue at the source.

knowledge_center.title: Use constitutional AI principles to self-critique agent outputs
knowledge_center.summary: Before returning a final answer, have the agent critique its own response against a checklist. This catches hallucinations and off-topic answers without human review.
body: LLM outputs are not always accurate or on-topic. A second-pass self-critique step catches obvious errors before they reach the user.

**Tip:** After generating an answer, run a critique pass with a simple checklist prompt.

```python
critique_prompt = """
Review the following answer and check:
1. Does it directly answer the question asked?
2. Are there any claims that seem uncertain or potentially hallucinated?
3. Is the tone appropriate?

Question: {question}
Answer: {answer}

Respond with: PASS if all criteria are met, or REVISE: <specific issue> if not.
"""

critique = llm.invoke(critique_prompt)
if critique.startswith('REVISE'):
    answer = llm.invoke(f'Revise this answer: {answer}\nIssue: {critique}')
```
This two-step generate-then-critique pattern adds one LLM call but reduces hallucination-related user complaints significantly in production.

knowledge_center.title: Use Pydantic models as tool input schemas for automatic validation
knowledge_center.summary: Defining tool inputs as Pydantic models gives you free input validation, type coercion, and auto-generated JSON schemas for the LLM — all in one step.
body: Writing tool input validation manually is error-prone and verbose. Pydantic does it automatically and also generates the JSON schema the LLM needs to call your tool correctly.
The schema is automatically serialized and included in the tool description sent to the LLM. The LLM then fills fields by name, and Pydantic validates before your function runs.

knowledge_center.title: Ollama now supports OpenAI-compatible API — run local models in any LangChain project
knowledge_center.summary: Ollama's new OpenAI-compatible endpoint means you can swap cloud LLMs for local models (Llama 3, Mistral, Phi-3) with a single URL change — no code refactoring.
body: Ollama added an OpenAI-compatible REST API at `http://localhost:11434/v1`. Any code using the OpenAI SDK or LangChain's ChatOpenAI can now point to a local model.

**What this means:** You can run agents locally for free during development, then switch to cloud for production — without changing a line of agent code.

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# Local Llama 3 via Ollama — OpenAI-compatible endpoint
llm = ChatOpenAI(
    model='llama3',
    base_url='http://localhost:11434/v1',
    api_key='ollama'  # any string works
)

# Everything else works identically
response = llm.invoke('Explain RAG in one sentence')
```
This makes local development and testing essentially free — no API costs while iterating on prompts and agent logic.

knowledge_center.title: Build agent evals before building the agent — not after
knowledge_center.summary: Most teams build the agent first and then write evals. Flipping this order — eval-first — forces clarity on success criteria and prevents scope creep.
body: Without clear eval criteria, you don't know when the agent is 'done'. You keep tweaking prompts without a signal telling you if things improved or regressed.

**Tip:** Before writing any agent code, define your eval set: 20–50 input/expected-output pairs that represent real user tasks.

```python
eval_cases = [
    {'input': 'What is our refund policy?', 'expected_contains': 'within 30 days'},
    {'input': 'How do I reset my password?', 'expected_contains': 'Settings > Security'},
    # ... 18 more
]

def run_eval(agent, cases):
    results = []
    for case in cases:
        output = agent.run(case['input'])
        passed = case['expected_contains'].lower() in output.lower()
        results.append({'input': case['input'], 'passed': passed})
    return sum(r['passed'] for r in results) / len(results)
```
Running evals before and after every prompt change gives you a concrete accuracy score instead of vibes. This is the single practice that most improved my agent development velocity.

knowledge_center.title: Compress retrieved chunks with extractive summarization before LLM injection
knowledge_center.summary: Raw retrieved chunks often contain boilerplate, repetition, and irrelevant sentences. Stripping these down before LLM injection cuts context tokens by 40–60%.
body: A 500-token retrieved chunk might contain only 150 tokens of truly relevant information. Passing the full chunk wastes tokens and dilutes the signal for the LLM.

**Trick:** Use extractive summarization to pull out the most relevant sentences from each chunk before injecting.

```python
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

relevance_scorer = CrossEncoder('cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2')

def extract_relevant_sentences(query: str, chunk: str, top_n: int = 3) -> str:
    sentences = chunk.split('. ')
    pairs = [(query, s) for s in sentences]
    scores = relevance_scorer.predict(pairs)
    top_sentences = [s for _, s in sorted(zip(scores, sentences), reverse=True)[:top_n]]
    return '. '.join(top_sentences)
```
On a 10-chunk retrieval, this cuts injected context from ~5,000 tokens to ~1,500 tokens with minimal information loss — a significant cost and quality improvement.

knowledge_center.title: LangChain LCEL chains returning empty output silently
knowledge_center.summary: If your LCEL chain returns an empty string or None without errors, the issue is usually a broken pipe between steps. Here's how to add visibility with pass-through inspection.
body: LCEL chains fail silently when an intermediate step returns an unexpected type. The next step might receive `None` and produce an empty output — no exception, no warning.

**Fix:** Insert a `RunnableLambda` debug step to inspect intermediate values.

```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def debug(x):
    print(f'[DEBUG] Type: {type(x)} | Value: {str(x)[:200]}')
    return x

chain = (
    retriever
    | RunnableLambda(debug)  # inspect what retriever returns
    | format_docs
    | RunnableLambda(debug)  # inspect what format_docs returns
    | llm
)
```
Remove the debug steps once the chain is working. This is faster than adding print statements throughout your codebase.

knowledge_center.title: Use graph-based indexing for multi-hop reasoning in RAG
knowledge_center.summary: Standard RAG fails for questions that require connecting information across multiple documents. Graph-based RAG (GraphRAG) handles multi-hop queries natively.
body: Questions like 'How does our CTO's previous company compare to our main competitor?' require connecting two separate documents — something vector search can't do in one hop.

**Tip:** Use Microsoft's GraphRAG or LlamaIndex's knowledge graph index for multi-hop queries.

```python
from llama_index.core import KnowledgeGraphIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader

documents = SimpleDirectoryReader('./docs').load_data()

kg_index = KnowledgeGraphIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    max_triplets_per_chunk=10,
)

query_engine = kg_index.as_query_engine(
    include_text=True,
    response_mode='tree_summarize'
)
response = query_engine.query('How did the 2023 acquisition affect our product roadmap?')
```
Graph RAG is slower and more expensive to build than a flat vector store, but for complex analytical queries over interconnected documents, it's the right architecture.

knowledge_center.title: Use async tool execution to parallelize independent agent tool calls
knowledge_center.summary: When an agent needs to call two independent tools, running them sequentially doubles latency. Async parallel execution cuts it in half.
body: An agent that fetches from a database AND searches the web needs both results to form its answer. Sequential execution means you wait for one before starting the other.

**Trick:** Detect independent tool calls and execute them concurrently with `asyncio.gather()`.

```python
import asyncio

async def parallel_tools(tool_calls: list) -> list:
    tasks = [execute_tool_async(call['name'], call['args']) for call in tool_calls]
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return list(results)

# Usage
tool_calls = [
    {'name': 'web_search', 'args': {'query': 'latest AI news'}},
    {'name': 'db_query', 'args': {'sql': 'SELECT * FROM reports LIMIT 5'}}
]
results = asyncio.run(parallel_tools(tool_calls))
```
For two tools with 2-second latency each, sequential takes 4 seconds. Parallel takes ~2 seconds. With 5 tools, the gap becomes even larger.

knowledge_center.title: OpenAI Realtime API now supports function calling for voice agents
knowledge_center.summary: OpenAI's Realtime API now lets you define tools that the voice model can call mid-conversation — enabling voice agents that can query databases, book appointments, and trigger workflows.
body: Previously, the Realtime API was limited to pure voice-to-voice conversation. Tool calling support turns it into a full voice agent platform.
The model can now ask for a customer's email, call your CRM, and respond with order details — all within a natural voice conversation, with no human in the loop.

knowledge_center.title: Add a fallback chain when the primary LLM is rate-limited or down
knowledge_center.summary: A single LLM provider is a single point of failure. Build a fallback chain that switches to an alternative provider when the primary returns an error.
body: At scale, you will hit rate limits. If your entire product depends on one LLM endpoint, a 30-second rate limit means 30 seconds of downtime for every user.

**Tip:** Use LangChain's `with_fallbacks()` to chain providers.

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

primary = ChatOpenAI(model='gpt-4o')
fallback = ChatAnthropic(model='claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022')

resilient_llm = primary.with_fallbacks([fallback])

# Automatically uses fallback if primary throws RateLimitError or APIError
response = resilient_llm.invoke('Summarize this document')
```
You can chain multiple fallbacks: `primary.with_fallbacks([fallback_1, fallback_2])`. The chain tries each in order until one succeeds.

knowledge_center.title: Use speculative decoding to speed up inference on self-hosted models
knowledge_center.summary: Speculative decoding uses a small draft model to propose tokens that a larger model verifies in parallel — cutting generation latency by 2–3x with identical output quality.
body: For self-hosted models (via Ollama, vLLM, or HuggingFace), speculative decoding is the most impactful inference optimization available.

**Trick:** Enable speculative decoding in vLLM by specifying a draft model.

```bash
python -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model meta-llama/Llama-3-70b-instruct \
    --speculative-model meta-llama/Llama-3-8b-instruct \
    --num-speculative-tokens 5
```

The 8B draft model proposes 5 tokens at a time. The 70B model validates them in a single forward pass — much faster than generating them one by one. On long outputs, this typically achieves 2–3x throughput improvement.

knowledge_center.title: Agent giving inconsistent answers to the same question on different runs
knowledge_center.summary: Non-deterministic agent behavior makes testing and debugging nearly impossible. Here's a checklist of the most common causes and fixes.
body: If your agent gives different answers to the same question, the sources of non-determinism are: temperature > 0, non-deterministic tool results, or random retrieval ordering.

**Fix checklist:**

```python
# 1. Set temperature=0 on all LLMs in the chain
llm = ChatOpenAI(model='gpt-4o', temperature=0)

# 2. Fix random seeds in vector search where supported
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=5)  # k is deterministic if vectors are

# 3. Sort tool results before injecting into prompt
docs.sort(key=lambda x: x.metadata['id'])  # consistent ordering

# 4. Log the exact prompt sent to the LLM for debugging
print('Exact prompt:', messages)
```
After fixing all four, run the same input 10 times and check for variance. Remaining variance usually points to a non-deterministic external API your tools call.

knowledge_center.title: Store embeddings with metadata rich enough to reconstruct the context
knowledge_center.summary: When you retrieve a chunk, you often need its surrounding context — document title, section, page number. Store this in metadata at index time, not retrieval time.
body: A common mistake is indexing only the chunk text. When the chunk is retrieved, you have no idea where it came from, what document it's part of, or whether it's current.

**Tip:** Enrich metadata at index time with everything you'll need at retrieval time.

```python
from langchain.schema import Document

doc = Document(
    page_content=chunk_text,
    metadata={
        'source': 'employee_handbook_v3.pdf',
        'page': 14,
        'section': 'Leave Policy',
        'last_updated': '2025-01-15',
        'chunk_index': 3,
        'doc_id': 'handbook-v3'
    }
)
```
At retrieval time, you can then cite the source, check freshness, filter by section, and provide document-level context to the LLM — all without any extra queries.

knowledge_center.title: Use LLM-as-judge for automated agent quality evaluation
knowledge_center.summary: Manually reviewing agent outputs doesn't scale. Using a powerful LLM to judge the quality of another LLM's output gives you a scalable, automated eval pipeline.
body: You can't manually review 10,000 agent responses. LLM-as-judge uses a model (usually GPT-4o) to score responses against a rubric — scalable, consistent, and surprisingly accurate.
Run this across your entire eval set nightly. Track score trends over time to catch regressions caused by prompt changes or model updates.

knowledge_center.title: Google released Gemini 2.0 Flash — fastest long-context model available
knowledge_center.summary: Gemini 2.0 Flash offers a 1M token context window at very low cost and high speed — making it the best option for bulk document processing and long-context extraction tasks.
body: Gemini 2.0 Flash supports up to 1 million input tokens, processes them faster than any comparable model, and is priced lower than GPT-4o-mini for most workloads.
**Best use cases:** Processing entire codebases, analyzing long transcripts, or extracting structured data from large PDFs — tasks where you'd otherwise need chunking.

```python
from langchain_google_genai import ChatGoogleGenerativeAI

llm = ChatGoogleGenerativeAI(
    model='gemini-2.0-flash',
    google_api_key=GOOGLE_API_KEY
)

# Pass an entire codebase as context
response = llm.invoke(f'Find all potential SQL injection vulnerabilities in:\n\n{entire_codebase}')
```
At 1M context, you can pass 750,000 words in a single call. For tasks where losing information to chunking is costly, this changes the architecture entirely.

knowledge_center.title: Implement exponential backoff for all LLM API calls in production
knowledge_center.summary: Rate limit errors are inevitable in production. Without retry logic, they become user-facing errors. Exponential backoff handles them gracefully.
body: LLM APIs rate-limit by tokens-per-minute, requests-per-minute, or both. Without retry logic, any spike in traffic causes cascading failures.
This retries up to 5 times with delays of 2, 4, 8, 16, 32 seconds. The exponential backoff gives the API time to recover from rate limiting without overwhelming it.

knowledge_center.title: Use task decomposition to break complex agent goals into verifiable subtasks
knowledge_center.summary: Asking an agent to do a complex 5-step task in one shot leads to errors that compound. Breaking it into verifiable subtasks with checkpoints prevents error propagation.
body: Complex single-shot tasks let errors compound silently — the agent gets step 2 wrong, and steps 3–5 all build on that wrong foundation.
This plan-then-execute-then-verify loop is the foundation of reliable agent systems. Each checkpoint catches errors before they contaminate the next step.

knowledge_center.title: Use delta streaming to reduce time-to-first-token in user-facing agents
knowledge_center.summary: Time-to-first-token (TTFT) matters more than total latency for user experience. Streaming with incremental rendering gets something on screen in under 500ms.
body: A 5-second wait with nothing on screen feels broken. A 5-second wait where text starts appearing in 300ms feels fast. TTFT is the UX metric that matters most.

**Tip:** Combine server-sent events (SSE) in FastAPI with client-side streaming render.

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()

@app.post('/chat')
async def chat(message: str):
    async def generate():
        stream = client.chat.completions.create(
            model='gpt-4o', messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': message}], stream=True
        )
        for chunk in stream:
            delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content
            if delta:
                yield f'data: {delta}\n\n'
    return StreamingResponse(generate(), media_type='text/event-stream')
```
On the frontend, handle `EventSource` and append each delta to the DOM as it arrives. This pattern is how ChatGPT, Claude.ai, and every major AI chat UI works.

knowledge_center.title: Chroma vector store getting slower over time — missing index rebuild
knowledge_center.summary: Chroma's HNSW index doesn't auto-optimize after bulk inserts. Query performance degrades significantly after 10K+ vectors without a manual index rebuild.
body: After inserting 50,000+ vectors into a local Chroma collection, query times that started at 20ms can creep up to 500ms+ because the HNSW index isn't automatically rebuilt.

**Fix:** Periodically rebuild the index by recreating the collection from scratch with the existing data.

```python
import chromadb

def rebuild_chroma_index(collection_name: str, client: chromadb.Client):
    old_collection = client.get_collection(collection_name)
    # Export all data
    data = old_collection.get(include=['embeddings', 'documents', 'metadatas'])
    # Delete and recreate
    client.delete_collection(collection_name)
    new_collection = client.create_collection(collection_name)
    # Reimport in batches
    batch_size = 1000
    for i in range(0, len(data['ids']), batch_size):
        new_collection.add(
            ids=data['ids'][i:i+batch_size],
            embeddings=data['embeddings'][i:i+batch_size],
            documents=data['documents'][i:i+batch_size],
            metadatas=data['metadatas'][i:i+batch_size]
        )
```
Schedule this rebuild during low-traffic periods. For production at scale, consider migrating to Pinecone or Weaviate which handle index optimization automatically.

knowledge_center.title: Use adaptive retrieval — query vector store only when needed
knowledge_center.summary: Not every user question requires a RAG lookup. Adding a routing step that skips retrieval for general questions saves latency and reduces irrelevant context injection.
body: A RAG pipeline that retrieves on every query injects potentially irrelevant context for general questions like 'What is machine learning?' — wasting tokens and sometimes confusing the model.
Use a small, fast model (gpt-4o-mini) for the routing step. The latency overhead is ~100ms but saves 500ms+ on queries that don't need retrieval.

knowledge_center.title: Version your prompts like code — use git and semantic versioning
knowledge_center.summary: Prompts are code. A random edit to a production system prompt can silently break agent behavior. Treat prompts with the same discipline as code: version control, changelog, rollback.
body: Prompt changes are the #1 cause of silent regressions in AI systems. 'I just tweaked the wording a little' can completely change agent behavior in edge cases.

**Tip:** Store prompts in version-controlled files with semantic versioning, not hardcoded strings.

```python
# prompts/system_v2.1.0.txt
# CHANGELOG: v2.1.0 - Added explicit instruction to cite sources
# v2.0.0 - Rewrote persona section

def load_prompt(name: str, version: str) -> str:
    path = f'prompts/{name}_v{version}.txt'
    with open(path) as f:
        return f.read()

system_prompt = load_prompt('system', '2.1.0')
```
Tag releases in git. When an agent starts behaving oddly, `git diff` shows exactly what changed and `git checkout` can roll back instantly — just like code.

knowledge_center.title: Use text compression (LLMLingua) to fit more context in fewer tokens
knowledge_center.summary: LLMLingua is a Microsoft tool that compresses long prompts by removing unnecessary words — achieving 5–20x compression with minimal quality loss.
body: When you're hitting context limits, the standard move is to remove content. LLMLingua takes a smarter approach: compress the existing content without removing information.

**Trick:** Use LLMLingua to compress retrieved RAG context before injection.

```python
from llmlingua import PromptCompressor

compressor = PromptCompressor(
    model_name='microsoft/llmlingua-2-xlm-roberta-large-meetingbank',
    use_llmlingua2=True
)

compressed = compressor.compress_prompt(
    context_text,
    rate=0.3,  # keep 30% of original tokens
    force_tokens=['\n', '?']  # always keep these
)

print(f'Original: {compressed["origin_tokens"]} tokens')
print(f'Compressed: {compressed["compressed_tokens"]} tokens')
```
At 0.3 rate, 3,000 tokens of context becomes ~900 tokens. In practice, RAG quality drops by 5–10% — an acceptable trade-off when you're hard up against context limits.
